questions.stem: An app needs a database that survives an AZ failure with automatic failover and no application code changes. Which option fits best?

options.explanation: Correct. Multi-AZ keeps a synchronous standby in another AZ and fails over automatically using the same endpoint.

options.text: RDS read replica in another AZ
options.explanation: Wrong. Read replicas serve reads and need manual promotion; failover isn't automatic.

options.text: A single-AZ RDS instance with daily snapshots
options.explanation: Wrong. Snapshots allow restore but not automatic failover, and an AZ outage causes downtime.

options.text: Storing the DB on an EBS volume with a backup
options.explanation: Wrong. EBS is AZ-scoped; an AZ failure takes the volume offline with no automatic failover.

questions.stem: An EC2 app must read from an S3 bucket. What is the most secure way to grant access?

options.text: Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance
options.explanation: Correct. Instance roles deliver temporary, rotated credentials with no long-lived keys to leak.

options.text: Store an access key and secret in the app config
options.explanation: Wrong. Long-lived static keys in config are a leading credential-leak risk.
Wrong. Public buckets expose data to everyone — a serious security misconfiguration.
Wrong. Root credentials should never be used by applications; they carry unrestricted access.

questions.stem: A read-heavy web app sees repeated database queries for the same data. Which AWS service best reduces DB load with minimal changes?

options.explanation: Correct. An in-memory cache (Redis/Memcached) absorbs repeated reads and offloads the database.
Wrong. Kinesis handles streaming ingestion, not read caching.
Wrong. Glue is ETL/data-catalog tooling, irrelevant to query caching.
Wrong. SQS decouples via queues; it doesn't cache read results.

questions.stem: A batch job is fault-tolerant and can restart if interrupted. Which purchase option minimizes compute cost?

options.explanation: Correct. Spot offers the deepest discount and suits interruptible, restartable workloads.
Wrong. On-Demand is flexible but the most expensive per hour for steady batch use.
Wrong. Dedicated Hosts target licensing/compliance needs at premium cost.
Wrong. RIs save on steady long-term usage, not short interruptible batches.

questions.stem: You must decouple a spike-prone front end from slower back-end processing so no requests are lost. Which service fits?

options.explanation: Correct. SQS buffers messages so the back end consumes at its own pace without dropping requests.
Wrong. Route 53 is DNS; it doesn't buffer workloads.
Wrong. WAF filters web traffic for security; it isn't a decoupling buffer.
Wrong. CloudFront caches/edge-delivers content; it doesn't queue back-end work.

questions.stem: Which measures help protect data at rest in S3? (Select TWO)

options.text: Enable SSE-KMS encryption on the bucket
options.explanation: Correct. Server-side encryption with KMS encrypts objects at rest with managed keys.
Correct. Block Public Access prevents accidental public exposure, protecting the data.

options.text: Put the bucket behind a NAT gateway
options.explanation: Wrong. NAT gateways route outbound VPC traffic; they don't apply to S3 object protection.

options.text: Attach a security group to the bucket
options.explanation: Wrong. S3 buckets don't use security groups; access is governed by policies and ACLs.
